title: The First Sermon
paragraphs:
Aldmoor in the morning was not a ruin. That was the worst thing about it. Sister Vess rode in through the west gate at the hour when the smoke from the breakfast fires should have been rising, and the smoke was rising. Bread was being baked. A dog was barking at a cart. A pair of old women were sweeping their stoop and complaining, audibly, about the rain.

It was almost right. It was very nearly right. And that gap between nearly and right was what made the back of Vess's neck go cold.

The old women looked up as she passed. They smiled at her. Both of them. At the same moment. With the same angle of the head.

"Blessings, sister," they said together.

"Blessings," said Sister Vess, and rode on, and did not turn around, and knew without turning that they were watching her go and that they had not stopped smiling.

She stabled her horse at an inn whose keeper, a man named Orrin, tried very hard to be ordinary and almost managed. He took her coin. He gave her a room. His hand brushed hers when he passed the key, and his skin, just for a moment, felt wrong. Not cold. Not hot. Textured. Like the soft underside of a mushroom just before it bruises.

She did not flinch. She had learned, a long time ago, not to flinch.

"Is there a sermon today, Master Orrin?"

"Oh, yes, sister." His eyes shone. "Every day there is a sermon. Everyone goes. You will go too."

It was not an invitation. She noted that it was not an invitation.

"What time," she said.

"When the bell rings. The Shepherd rings it himself. He says, a bell is a mouth the town shares."

She looked at him for a long beat. He looked back at her with the calm, slightly amused patience of a man waiting for a child to understand a simple sum.

"I see," she said.

She did not see. She did not want to see.
The bell rang at the second hour after noon.

It was not a normal bell. Sister Vess had grown up under the bells of the Crown Church and knew their languages — the low iron of the death-bell, the bright copper of the wedding-bell, the three-and-one of the warning. This was none of those. This bell had a single note, soft and wet, as if it had been cast out of something that was not metal. When she heard it, she found her feet moving toward the chapel before her mind had given the order.

She stopped walking, deliberately, in the middle of the square. She counted to thirty.

She made her feet start again only when she had decided to make them.
That was what she told herself.

The chapel was too small for the crowd, but everybody fit anyway.

That was what she could not stop looking at. Three hundred people were standing inside a building meant for ninety. They were not packed. They stood with comfortable space between them. But the walls of the chapel had not moved, and the ceiling had not lifted, and yet the air was light and the aisles were wide and somehow, impossibly, when she looked at any given pew she could not be sure how many people were sitting in it without counting, and each time she counted, she got a different answer.

At the front, on what had been Father Aulen's altar, stood the Shepherd.

He was not impressive. That was his trick. Sister Vess had expected a figure and been given a man.

He was of middle height. His robes were plain and yellowed. His beard was dark and knotted, and a single braid hung down through it weighted with a small smooth river stone. His hands were folded in front of him. He was not, she noticed, wet, although he had walked to the chapel in the rain.

He saw her the moment she came in.

It was not that he looked at her. It was that she understood, as if he had told her patiently, that he had been looking at her already for some time, and was pleased that she had finally joined him.
"Sister," he said. His voice carried into every corner of the chapel without effort, without being loud. "Sit. You have had a long ride."

There was a bench. She had not noticed the bench before. It had not been there before. She knew, with absolute certainty and no evidence, that it had not been there before. She sat on it anyway, because to keep standing would have been to admit, in front of three hundred witnesses, that she was afraid.
The sermon was, on its face, a kind one.

The Shepherd spoke about the rain. He said the rain was a gift. He said the world above had been dry for a very long time and had grown brittle, and that brittle things break at the joints, and that the rain was softening the world so that it could be remade without breakage. He said this gently, the way a doctor explains a difficult kindness to a patient. He used the word we a great deal. He said: we are the soil now. He said: we are the quiet green thing, patient under the leaves. He said: the old gods are out there still, shouting on their dry stone thrones, but they cannot hear us, because we no longer speak in the language of stone. We speak in the language of root and damp and waiting.

Sister Vess, sitting on the bench that had not existed a moment ago, found herself listening.

She found the listening pleasant.

She found the listening pleasant in the same way that lying down in a warm bath is pleasant after a very long journey. She found the pleasantness terrifying. She fixed her eyes on the small smooth river stone in the Shepherd's beard and made herself recite, silently and in order, the seventeen names of every person she had interceded for in the last nine years, beginning with the heretic-cook of Marrow Bridge and ending with the boy-saint of Ember Fen. She got to the seventh name before the listening stopped being pleasant.
She got to the eleventh before the Shepherd, without turning his face toward her, said aloud: "Marrow Bridge. Yes. I remember her. She was very brave. You were very kind."

After the sermon the congregation rose and filed out into the rain, one by one, without speaking, the way polite guests leave a funeral. Each of them, as they passed the altar, laid a small thing on it. A coin. A ribbon. A child's milk-tooth. A folded piece of paper. A lock of hair. A tiny, clear vial of something pale and green.

Sister Vess did not rise.

She sat on her bench until the chapel was empty except for herself and the Shepherd and the little mountain of offerings.

The Shepherd looked at her kindly. He had a very kind face.

"You should not come to the next one," he said. "Not yet. I think the next one would be too much for you. Come on the seventh day. By then you will be ready."

"I am here to kill you," said Sister Vess.

"I know," said the Shepherd. "That is why I said the seventh day. By the seventh day, you won't want to anymore. And that will be better for both of us. Go and rest, sister. The room at Orrin's is warm. I had him light the fire."

She stood. She crossed the chapel. She did not hurry. At the door she turned, because some small, hard, seventeen-year-trained part of her refused to walk out without a last look.

The Shepherd was still watching her. The little mountain of offerings on the altar had, while her back was turned, begun, very faintly, to move.
